---
title: "The Setlist"
subtitle: "Day 9 · Robot Rockstars · SPIKE Prime + Pybricks"
date: today
format: revealjs
---

# A. Warm-Up {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

Your library is one clean module and it survived the gauntlet. Today the *mission itself* becomes a thing you can hold, reorder, and grow. 🎵

## Where we left off

| You have | What it does |
|---|---|
| 📦 **one `my_lib.py`** | every verb in one module — one constants block tunes them all |
| 🚗 **moving verbs** | `drive_straight` · `turn_to` — watched by *your* PD loop |
| 🧱 **wall verbs** | `drive_to_wall` · `wall_square` — the motor as a touch sensor |
| 👀 **line verbs** | `follow_line` · `square_on_line` — the mat as a map |

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
🤔 The gauntlet proved every verb works *alone*. A competition run is verbs **in a row** — and the row keeps changing. Today: how pros hold a plan that has to change in seconds.
:::

⏱️ **Time: 10 minutes**

::: {.notes}
Recap by asking: name the verbs, who tuned what, which constant fixes which move. The framing sentence for the day: your library is the *vocabulary*; today you get the *sentence*. Foreshadow the pain honestly — the judge at the end of today will call a mission and then change it, and squads whose plan is a pasted block will lose minutes where a list squad loses seconds. That's the break-it, and it's also exactly what the Surprise Rule does at a real WRO table.
:::

# B. The Plan That Couldn't Change {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #FF7A5C)"}

Your verbs work. Now the judge calls a *sequence* — and then changes it. 😈

## Unplugged: the pigeonholes 📮

::: {.qbox}
[Six numbered boxes on the bench, a pile of colored bricks. "Red into box 0. Blue into box 2. What's in box 1? Swap 0 and 3. Add a green one at the end — which box did it land in?"]{.qtitle}
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.cardbox .teal}
You just used every list skill: a **numbered row of slots**, filled in order, **counting from 0** — read a slot by its number (**index**), walk the row front to back (**iterate**), grow it at the end (**append**). Python calls this row a **list**. No laptop needed — you already think this way.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Run it physically before any code — 5 minutes, real boxes (or taped squares) and real bricks, quick-fire calls. The two stumbles to engineer on purpose: (1) "box 1" when they expect counting from 1 — let someone grab the wrong brick, then name the rule: robots count from 0; (2) append lands at the *end*, position = old length. Keep the boxes on the bench all day — every list question later gets answered by pointing at them. This is WRO-Learn's arrays intro compressed into a game.
:::

## Break it: the pasted plan 🔥 {.code-shrink}

::: {.qbox}
[The judge calls: *cross the stage · face the band · square on the wall.* You paste it as one block. Then: **"Actually — wall square FIRST."** How long does the edit take?]{.qtitle}
:::

```{.python code-line-numbers="|1-5|6-7"}
# mission_friday.py — one pasted block 😬
drive_straight(400)          # cross the stage
turn_to(90)           # face the band
drive_to_wall()
wall_square()         # reset at the wall
# judge: "wall square FIRST, then cross"…
# select lines… which? cut… paste… 🐛
```

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.cardbox .coral}
The plan is **trapped in the code**. Reordering means surgery — select, cut, paste, and pray you moved *all* the lines of each step. Under a 5-minute clock, surgery loses. The plan needs to be a **thing** you can shuffle — like cards in a deck.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Let a squad actually attempt the live edit under a visible countdown — the fumble is the lesson. Diagnose together: the *steps* are clear in their heads ("cross", "face", "square") but the code has no such boundaries; a step is 1–3 loose lines with nothing holding them together. Two missing ideas, teased in order: give each step a **name** (they own `def` — this is a two-minute step), then put the names **in a row that can be shuffled** — the pigeonholes from the warm-up. Don't show the fix yet.
:::

# C. The Mission Is a List {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #625D9C)"}

Name the steps. Put the names in a row. Play the row.

## Lists in sixty seconds

```{.python code-line-numbers="|1|2|3|4|5-6"}
setlist = ["intro", "solo", "finale"]   # a numbered row of slots
setlist[0]                # "intro" — count from 0, like the boxes!
setlist.append("encore")  # grows at the end → slot 3
setlist[1] = "duet"       # swap what a slot holds
for song in setlist:      # visit every slot, front to back
    print(song)
```

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.cardbox .teal}
Index · append · iterate — the three moves from the pigeonhole game, now in Python. A list holds **anything**: strings, numbers… and (next slide) something sneakier. 😏
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Type it live in the REPL, mirroring each line back to the physical boxes on the bench ("slot 0… that box"). Off-by-one and negative index (`setlist[-1]` = last box) are fun 30-second asides if the room is quick. Don't linger — the payoff slide is next, and lists only get interesting once functions go inside them.
:::

## Steps are just functions — hold the name, don't play it

```{.python code-line-numbers="|1-2|4-5|7-8|10-11"}
def cross_stage():
    drive_straight(400)

def face_band():
    turn_to(90)

def reset_at_wall():
    drive_to_wall(); wall_square()

mission = [reset_at_wall, cross_stage, face_band]
#          ^ no parentheses — cards in a deck, not moves happening
```

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.cardbox .purple}
`cross_stage()` **plays** the move. `cross_stage` — no parentheses — is the **recipe card itself**, a thing you can hold, hand around… and drop into a list. A list of names is a **setlist**: the whole show, and it hasn't started yet.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the day's one genuinely new idea — a function is a *value*. Land it with the recipe-card metaphor from the first `def` day: the card vs cooking the dish. Common bug to demo on purpose: write `mission = [cross_stage(), face_band()]` — the robot drives *while the list is being built* (the moves happen at line 10!) and the list ends up holding three `None`s. Let them see that, then delete the parentheses. Note the judge's reorder is now already solved on line 10 — `reset_at_wall` simply moved to slot 0.
:::

## The runner — the whole thing

```{.python code-line-numbers="|1|2|3|5"}
def run_mission(steps):
    for step in steps:   # take the next card…
        step()           # …now play it
run_mission(mission)
```

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.cardbox .teal}
Three lines, and they **never change again**. Reorder the show → edit the *list*. Insert a step → `append` or slot it in. The judge changes the mission; you change **data**, not code. That's the split the pros live by.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Read it aloud in pigeonhole language: for each box front to back, take the card out, play it. The deep point — worth saying explicitly, once: the runner is *control* and the mission is *data*, and everything that changes lives in the data. This exact runner drives the anchor run in Week 3; per-step timeouts and a transition log are its stretch upgrade (the exemplar's `run_mission_logged`) — offer to fast squads at the gate, never gate on it. A non-blocking state machine is continuation-season material; RoboMission runs are sequential, blocking is right.
:::

## Feel it: shuffle the setlist 🔀

```{=html}
<div style="width:860px;margin:0 auto;">
  <div style="display:flex;justify-content:center;gap:12px;margin-bottom:8px;">
    <button onclick="mlRun()" style="background:#00C9A7;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:10px;padding:8px 20px;font-size:19px;font-weight:700;cursor:pointer;">▶ run_mission(mission)</button>
    <button onclick="mlSwap()" style="background:#625D9C;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:10px;padding:8px 20px;font-size:19px;font-weight:700;cursor:pointer;">🔀 judge: wall square FIRST</button>
    <button onclick="mlAppend()" style="background:#E96852;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:10px;padding:8px 20px;font-size:19px;font-weight:700;cursor:pointer;">➕ append(face_crowd)</button>
  </div>
  <div style="display:flex;justify-content:center;align-items:flex-start;gap:22px;">
    <div style="min-width:280px;">
      <div style="font-family:Consolas,monospace;font-size:16px;color:#1C355E;font-weight:800;margin-bottom:6px;">mission = [</div>
      <div id="mlList"></div>
      <div style="font-family:Consolas,monospace;font-size:16px;color:#1C355E;font-weight:800;margin-top:6px;">]</div>
    </div>
    <svg width="430" height="330" viewBox="0 0 430 330" style="display:block;background:#eef3f8;border-radius:14px;">
      <rect x="40" y="18" width="350" height="16" rx="4" fill="#625D9C"/>
      <text x="215" y="14" font-size="12" fill="#625D9C" font-weight="800" text-anchor="middle">wall</text>
      <circle cx="120" cy="270" r="7" fill="#1C355E"/>
      <text x="120" y="296" font-size="13" fill="#1C355E" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="700">start</text>
      <polyline id="mlTrace" points="" fill="none" stroke="#00C9A7" stroke-width="4" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
      <g id="mlBot" transform="translate(120,270) rotate(0)"><g transform="scale(0.55)">
        <rect x="-14" y="-30" width="28" height="54" rx="8" fill="#1C355E"/>
        <path d="M -8 -12 L 0 -23 L 8 -12 Z" fill="#fff" opacity="0.9"/>
        <circle cy="-30" r="4" fill="#FFD500"/>
      </g></g>
      <text id="mlMsg" x="215" y="322" font-size="15" fill="#1C355E" font-weight="800" text-anchor="middle"></text>
    </svg>
  </div>
</div>
<script>
(function(){
  var raf=null, last=null, running=false;
  var WALL_Y=52, BACK_Y=80;  // wall contact line and post-back-off line
  // step catalog: each named step expands into primitive phases
  // rot = turn to absolute heading (0 = up, 90 = right, 180 = down)
  // fwd = drive dist px along current heading · toY/backToY = drive straight up/down to a y line
  var STEPS={
    cross_stage:  {label:'cross_stage',   phases:function(){ return [{k:'fwd',d:130}]; }},
    face_band:    {label:'face_band',     phases:function(){ return [{k:'rot',h:90}]; }},
    reset_at_wall:{label:'reset_at_wall', phases:function(){ return [{k:'rot',h:0},{k:'toY',y:WALL_Y},{k:'pause',s:0.5},{k:'backToY',y:BACK_Y},{k:'rot',h:180}]; }},
    face_crowd:   {label:'face_crowd',    phases:function(){ return [{k:'rot',h:270}]; }}
  };
  var mission=['cross_stage','face_band','reset_at_wall'];
  var appended=false;
  function el(id){return document.getElementById(id);}
  function render(hi){
    var html='';
    for(var i=0;i<mission.length;i++){
      var on = i===hi;
      html+='<div style="font-family:Consolas,monospace;font-size:17px;padding:7px 14px;margin:4px 0 4px 18px;border-radius:10px;border-left:5px solid '+(on?'#00AE8D':'#c9d4e0')+';background:'+(on?'#00AE8D22':'#eef3f8')+';color:#1C355E;font-weight:'+(on?'800':'600')+';">'
        + i + ': ' + STEPS[mission[i]].label + (on?' ▶':'') + '</div>';
    }
    el('mlList').innerHTML=html;
  }
  render(-1);
  function animate(idx, phases, done){
    // robot state persists on the g transform; parse it back
    var m=/translate\(([-\d.]+),([-\d.]+)\) rotate\(([-\d.]+)\)/.exec(el('mlBot').getAttribute('transform'));
    var x=+m[1], y=+m[2], h=+m[3];
    render(idx);
    var pi=0, t=0; last=null;
    function trace(){ el('mlTrace').setAttribute('points', el('mlTrace').getAttribute('points')+' '+x.toFixed(1)+','+y.toFixed(1)); }
    function frame(now){
      var dt=last?Math.min((now-last)/1000,0.05):0.016; last=now; t+=dt;
      var p=phases[pi], fin=false, sp=150;
      if(p.k==='rot'){
        var diff=p.h-h;
        if(Math.abs(diff)<0.5){ h=p.h; fin=true; }
        else h+=Math.sign(diff)*Math.min(Math.abs(diff),200*dt);
      } else if(p.k==='fwd'){
        if(p.left===undefined) p.left=p.d;
        var step=Math.min(sp*dt,p.left), r=(h-90)*Math.PI/180;
        x+=step*Math.cos(r); y+=step*Math.sin(r); p.left-=step; trace();
        if(p.left<=0.1) fin=true;
      } else if(p.k==='toY'){
        y=Math.max(p.y, y-sp*dt); trace();
        if(y<=p.y+0.1) fin=true;
      } else if(p.k==='backToY'){
        y=Math.min(p.y, y+sp*dt); trace();
        if(y>=p.y-0.1) fin=true;
      } else { // pause (the squaring wiggle moment)
        if(p.done===undefined){ p.done=t+p.s; }
        if(t>=p.done) fin=true;
      }
      el('mlBot').setAttribute('transform','translate('+x.toFixed(1)+','+y.toFixed(1)+') rotate('+h.toFixed(1)+')');
      if(fin){ pi++; t=0; }
      if(pi<phases.length){ raf=requestAnimationFrame(frame); }
      else { raf=null; done(); }
    }
    raf=requestAnimationFrame(frame);
  }
  window.mlRun=function(){
    if(running) return; running=true;
    el('mlBot').setAttribute('transform','translate(120,270) rotate(0)');
    el('mlTrace').setAttribute('points','120,270');
    el('mlMsg').textContent='';
    var i=0;
    function next(){
      if(i>=mission.length){ running=false; render(-1); el('mlMsg').textContent='show complete 🎉 — same runner, whatever the list says'; return; }
      animate(i, STEPS[mission[i]].phases(), function(){ i++; next(); });
    }
    next();
  };
  window.mlSwap=function(){
    if(running) return;
    var i=mission.indexOf('reset_at_wall');
    if(i>0){ mission.splice(i,1); mission.unshift('reset_at_wall'); }
    render(-1); el('mlMsg').textContent='one edit to the LIST — the runner never changed 😎';
  };
  window.mlAppend=function(){
    if(running || appended) return; appended=true;
    mission.push('face_crowd');
    render(-1); el('mlMsg').textContent='mission.append(face_crowd) → new slot '+(mission.length-1);
  };
})();
</script>
```

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
::: {.cardbox .purple}
The judge's reorder is **one edit to the list** — watch slot 0 change hands, then run the *same* three-line runner. Append a step: a new slot appears at the end, exactly like the pigeonholes. **The plan is data now.**
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Ritual: run the original show first so they see list-highlighting track the robot (the ▶ walks down the slots as the robot moves — index made visible). Then hit the judge button *without* running: only the list changes, code untouched — say it out loud. Run again: wall square happens first. Append, run a third time. Connect each button to the pigeonhole verbs: swap = re-slotting cards, append = new box at the end. Then send them to the real mat to do the identical thing with their own verbs — the sim is the storyboard, the mat is the exam.
:::

# D. Compete & Gate {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

Today's showdown: **Setlist** 🎵

## Setlist 🎵

::: {.callout-important}
## The challenge
The judge calls a **3-step mini-mission** — you have **5 minutes** to encode it as a mission list over your own library and run it. Score = steps completed; tiebreak = time.
:::

- Hear the mission → say it as **verbs** (*"that's a `drive_straight`, a `turn_to`, a `wall_square`"*) → wrap, list, run.
- The judge **will** change the order on round two. 😈 Your surgery-free edit is the whole advantage.
- Fast squads: swap in `run_mission_logged` — per-step stopwatch + a printed transition log.

::: {.notes}
Score decomposition, not typing speed: the squads to praise are the ones who translate the judge's sentence into library verbs *before* touching a keyboard. Round two's reorder is the day's thesis under pressure — a list squad edits one line; watch for anyone still cutting and pasting blocks and coach them on the spot. The logged runner is the exemplar's stretch (timeouts + transition log) — offer it, never gate it. Coach's private scorecard today: list fluency, decomposition into verbs, debugging a *sequence* (which step failed? the log says).
:::

## Today's gate

::: {.callout-important}
## You're done today when…
You can **read and build a list** (index from 0, iterate, append), your steps are **named functions over your own library**, and the three-line runner walks a judge-called **3-step mission** — with a reorder costing you **one edit**, not surgery.
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
Next: **Draft Day** — teams form, each team picks its competition robot… and a surprise you've spent two weeks earning. 🎁
:::

::: {.notes}
The gate is the plan's D9 outcome verbatim. This is the last solo-robot day — tomorrow's session opens with the *Mini Mission* finale and the draft, so make sure every student's library + runner are saved and named (they carry into the team merge, which is exactly what motivates the config lesson there). Keep the teaser vague on purpose: the "surprise" is the DriveBase reveal, and it lands best unspoiled.
:::
